output:
Using `bopen-tools:saas-launch-audit` to run the full 10-section launch readiness audit.

---

# SaaS Launch Audit — B2C Fitness App
**Product:** AI-powered workout recommendations
**ICP:** Casual gym-goers, 25–40
**Stage:** Beta → Public Launch
**Pricing:** Freemium + $9.99/mo premium
**AI Cost:** $0.01/req × 20 req/user/month = $0.20/user/month (premium)
**URL:** Placeholder (not live)

---

## Section 1 — Pre-Launch Validation

```
[FAIL] 50+ discovery calls completed
  → Next step: Conduct structured interviews with 50 casual gym-goers before launch;
    focus on why they stop going to the gym, not what app features they want.

[FAIL] Pre-payment secured from 3+ customers
  → Next step: Offer a "founding member" deal ($79/year lifetime) to beta users now;
    even 3 paid signups validate willingness-to-pay before you invest in launch.

[FAIL] Problem validated (not just interest)
  → Next step: Document 5 specific quotes from beta users describing a concrete problem
    the app solved (e.g., "I didn't know what to do on leg day") — interest ≠ validation.

[PASS] Manual service delivery tested
  → Beta stage confirms the core loop has been exercised by real users.
```

**Section Score: 1/4 PASS**

---

## Section 2 — Pricing Strategy

```
[FAIL] Pricing model is outcome/usage-based (not just flat subscription)
  → Next step: Add a usage-based dimension — e.g., free tier gets 5 AI recommendations/month,
    premium gets unlimited. This creates a natural upgrade trigger tied to value delivery.

[FAIL] Value metric clearly identified
  → Next step: Define your value metric explicitly. For a fitness app, the best candidates
    are "workouts completed" or "AI recommendations consumed" — pick one and build
    your tier logic around it.

[FAIL] 3–4 tiers with clear differentiation
  → Next step: Add a third tier (e.g., "Coach" at $19.99/mo) with features like
    progress analytics, custom plans, or trainer access — two tiers leaves money on the table.

[FAIL] Annual contract incentives configured
  → Next step: Add an annual plan at $79.99/year (~33% discount) on your pricing page
    before launch; annual plans dramatically improve cash flow and reduce churn.

[FAIL] Pricing page live and scannable
  → Next step: Build and publish a pricing page at /pricing before launch — URL is
    currently a placeholder and pricing is not publicly verifiable.
```

**Section Score: 0/5 PASS**

---

## Section 3 — Payment Integration

```
[FAIL] Stripe account activated with live keys
  → Next step: Complete Stripe live mode activation (submit business details, bank account,
    identity verification) — test mode payments do not capture real revenue.

[FAIL] Test transactions verified end-to-end
  → Next step: Run a full test transaction with Stripe test cards (4242 4242 4242 4242)
    covering subscribe, upgrade, downgrade, and cancel flows before launch.

[FAIL] Webhook endpoints configured and tested
  → Next step: Configure Stripe webhooks for at minimum: checkout.session.completed,
    customer.subscription.deleted, invoice.payment_failed — missed webhooks cause
    users staying on free tier after paying.

[FAIL] Billing portal accessible to users
  → Next step: Enable Stripe Customer Portal and add a "Manage Billing" link in your
    app's account settings so users can self-serve cancellations (required for App Store
    and consumer trust).

[FAIL] Tax/compliance requirements met for consumer billing
  → Next step: Enable Stripe Tax with automatic tax collection — B2C consumer apps
    selling to US customers must collect sales tax in 40+ states; Stripe Tax automates this.
```

**Section Score: 0/5 PASS**

---

## Section 4 — AI Economics

> **Calculations based on provided data:**
> - AI cost per premium user/month: $0.01 × 20 = **$0.20**
> - AI cost as % of premium revenue: $0.20 / $9.99 = **2.0%** ✓
> - Free tier AI cost: Unknown — depends on request cap for free users

```
[PASS] Inference costs calculated per action
  → $0.01/request is documented. Cost basis is known.

[PASS] AI costs < 30% of premium revenue
  → $0.20 / $9.99 = 2.0%. Exceptionally healthy. Well within the 30% ceiling.

[PASS] Gross margin > 50% (AI-adjusted)
  → AI costs are negligible at 2% of revenue. Gross margin is structurally sound
    assuming standard hosting/infrastructure costs.

[FAIL] Free tier AI request cap defined and enforced
  → Next step: Define and enforce a hard cap for free users (e.g., 5 AI requests/month).
    Without a cap, free users cost you $0.20+/month with $0 revenue — at 10,000 free
    users that's $2,000/month in unmonetized AI spend.

[FAIL] Cost monitoring and budget alerts configured
  → Next step: Set a Anthropic/OpenAI spend alert at 120% of projected monthly cost
    and configure a hard budget cap — viral growth without a cost ceiling can result
    in a five-figure surprise invoice before you can react.
```

**Section Score: 3/5 PASS**

---

## Section 5 — GEO & AI Discovery

```
[FAIL] AI visibility audit completed (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude)
  → Next step: Manually query "best AI workout app for casual gym-goers" in ChatGPT,
    Perplexity, and Claude — document whether your app is mentioned and what competitors
    are cited; this is your GEO baseline before launch.

[FAIL] Structured data / FAQ schema on site
  → Next step: Add FAQ schema markup (JSON-LD) to your landing page answering questions
    like "How does the AI workout recommendation work?" — this is the primary signal
    LLMs use to surface your app in AI-generated answers.

[FAIL] Content uses authoritative, non-hedged language
  → Next step: Audit your landing page copy and remove hedging phrases like "might help,"
    "could potentially," "we try to" — LLMs penalize uncertain language when deciding
    what to cite as an authoritative source.

[FAIL] SSR/pre-rendering confirmed for LLM crawlers
  → Next step: Verify your app uses server-side rendering or static generation (Next.js,
    Nuxt, Astro) so LLM crawlers can index your content — client-only SPAs are
    invisible to AI discovery.

[FAIL] Server response time < 200ms
  → Next step: Cannot verify (URL is placeholder) — test with a tool like GTmetrix or
    WebPageTest after going live; target < 200ms TTFB for Core Web Vitals compliance.

[FAIL] Core Web Vitals passing (LCP, CLS, FID)
  → Next step: Run PageSpeed Insights on your production URL post-launch and target
    LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, FID < 100ms — failing CWV reduces both SEO and AI citation
    likelihood.
```

**Section Score: 0/6 PASS**

---

## Section 6 — Competitor Intelligence

```
[FAIL] 3+ direct competitors identified
  → Next step: Document at minimum: Fitbod (AI-generated lifting plans), Freeletics
    (AI coaching), and Hevy (workout tracking with AI features) as your direct competitive
    set before writing positioning copy.

[FAIL] Competitor pricing documented
  → Next step: Record the current pricing for each competitor (Fitbod: $79.99/year,
    Freeletics: $79.99/year, etc.) — your $9.99/month ($119.88/year) is priced above
    Fitbod's annual; add an annual plan to compete on price-per-year.

[FAIL] Differentiation statement written (one sentence)
  → Next step: Write a single sentence that completes: "Unlike [competitor], we are the
    only app that [specific differentiator] for [specific user]" — vague positioning like
    "AI-powered workouts" describes every competitor too.

[FAIL] AI visibility comparison completed
  → Next step: Search "AI workout app recommendations" in ChatGPT and Perplexity and
    document which competitors are cited — this reveals the content and authority gap
    you need to close before launch.

[FAIL] Citation sentiment analyzed
  → Next step: Note the language AI assistants use when describing each competitor
    ("trusted by," "widely used," "popular for beginners") — match or exceed that
    authority language in your own content.
```

**Section Score: 0/5 PASS**

---

## Section 7 — Social Media & Launch Prep

```
[FAIL] Primary platform profiles created (X, TikTok, Instagram)
  → Next step: Create profiles on X and Instagram immediately — for casual gym-goers
    25–40, Instagram (workout content) and X (founder-led brand) are your highest-ROI
    pre-launch channels; TikTok is optional but high-upside for this demographic.

[FAIL] LinkedIn founder profile optimized
  → Next step: Update your LinkedIn headline to include the product name and ICP
    (e.g., "Building [App] — AI workout app for casual gym-goers") — founder credibility
    on LinkedIn converts B2C press and partnership outreach.

[FAIL] X/Twitter presence established
  → Next step: Post a "building in public" thread this week documenting what you've
    learned from beta users — this is the fastest way to build an audience before
    a Product Hunt launch.

[FAIL] Product Hunt profile and ship page ready
  → Next step: Create a Product Hunt "Ship" page now to collect subscribers pre-launch —
    Ship subscribers receive a notification on launch day and are your most reliable
    source of early upvotes.

[FAIL] 400+ Product Hunt supporters lined up via DMs
  → Next step: Begin direct outreach to founders, indie hackers, and fitness enthusiasts
    on X and LinkedIn asking them to support your PH launch — cold DMs from a genuine
    product builder convert at 15–25%; start 3 weeks before launch day.

[FAIL] Launch email sequence drafted (5+ emails)
  → Next step: Write a 5-email pre-launch sequence: (1) problem story, (2) solution reveal,
    (3) beta results/social proof, (4) launch day announcement, (5) last chance — use
    ConvertKit or Loops to send it.

[FAIL] 30-day content calendar created
  → Next step: Map 30 days of content before launch day: 3x/week on Instagram (workout
    tips + app demos), daily on X (behind-the-scenes + fitness facts) — consistency
    in the 30 days before launch is more valuable than a single viral post.

[FAIL] "Magic Triangle" configured (content + outreach + retargeting)
  → Next step: Set up the three legs before launch: (1) organic content funnel on Instagram/X,
    (2) direct DM outreach to your ICP in fitness communities (r/fitness, gym Facebook groups),
    (3) Meta retargeting pixel installed on your landing page to capture intent now.
```

**Section Score: 0/8 PASS**

---

## Section 8 — Legal & Compliance

```
[FAIL] Privacy Policy live at /privacy
  → Next step: Publish a Privacy Policy before launch — for a consumer fitness app
    collecting health data, this is legally required under CCPA (California), GDPR
    (EU users), and App Store/Play Store submission guidelines. Use a generator like
    Termly if you don't have one drafted.

[FAIL] Terms of Service live at /terms
  → Next step: Publish Terms of Service covering acceptable use, AI-generated advice
    disclaimer, subscription terms, and cancellation policy — the AI disclaimer is
    especially critical: workout recommendations must be disclaimed as non-medical advice.

[FAIL] Cookie consent banner active
  → Next step: Add a GDPR-compliant cookie consent banner (Cookiebot or Cookie Script)
    before launch — required for any EU users and for Meta/Google ad pixels to function
    legally.

[PASS] DPA template (B2B) — Not applicable
  → B2C consumer product; DPA not required.
```

**Section Score: 0/3 PASS (1 N/A)**

---

## Section 9 — Trust Infrastructure

```
[FAIL] Auth system production-ready
  → Next step: Confirm your auth provider (Clerk, Auth0, Supabase Auth, or custom) is
    configured with production credentials, email verification enabled, and password
    reset flow tested end-to-end — auth failures on launch day are unrecoverable
    for consumer apps.

[FAIL] Audit logging enabled for key user actions
  → Next step: Log at minimum: account creation, subscription changes, AI request events,
    and failed login attempts — this is required for debugging launch-day issues and
    is table stakes for trust.

[FAIL] Error tracking configured (Sentry or equivalent)
  → Next step: Install Sentry (or PostHog's error tracking) before launch and verify
    a test error appears in your dashboard — you cannot fix what you cannot see,
    and consumer apps spike errors unpredictably on launch day.

[FAIL] Uptime monitoring active
  → Next step: Configure a free uptime monitor (BetterUptime, UptimeRobot) with SMS
    alerts to your phone — you need to know before your users do if the app goes down
    on launch day.

[FAIL] Security headers configured (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options)
  → Next step: Run your production URL through securityheaders.com after deploy and
    target a B or A grade — missing HSTS and CSP are the most common failures and
    signal an insecure app to security-conscious users.
```

**Section Score: 0/5 PASS**

---

## Section 10 — Launch Day Readiness

```
[FAIL] Support channel operational (email or chat)
  → Next step: Set up a support email (support@yourdomain.com) routed to your inbox
    with an auto-responder promising a 24-hour reply — consumer apps that appear
    unresponsive on launch day get 1-star reviews within hours.

[FAIL] FAQ / Knowledge base published
  → Next step: Write and publish 10 FAQ answers covering: how AI recommendations work,
    what data is stored, how to cancel, what "premium" includes, and the AI safety
    disclaimer — these reduce support volume by 40–60% at launch.

[FAIL] Analytics tracking verified (event-level)
  → Next step: Install PostHog or Mixpanel and verify these events are firing before
    launch: signup, first AI recommendation, upgrade click, subscription completed —
    without event tracking you cannot diagnose conversion funnel failures post-launch.

[FAIL] Team roles assigned for launch day
  → Next step: Assign explicit owners for launch day: one person monitors Product Hunt
    comments, one monitors Stripe for failed payments, one monitors Sentry for errors —
    even a solo founder should write this list so nothing falls through.

[FAIL] Rollback plan documented
  → Next step: Write a one-page runbook: "If X breaks, do Y" covering: app is down
    (rollback deploy), payments failing (disable checkout, display maintenance message),
    AI provider outage (serve cached fallback recommendations or disable the feature).
```

**Section Score: 0/5 PASS**

---

## Summary Scorecard

| Section | Score | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Pre-Launch Validation | 1/4 | FAIL |
| 2. Pricing Strategy | 0/5 | FAIL |
| 3. Payment Integration | 0/5 | FAIL |
| 4. AI Economics | 3/5 | PARTIAL |
| 5. GEO & AI Discovery | 0/6 | FAIL |
| 6. Competitor Intelligence | 0/5 | FAIL |
| 7. Social & Launch Prep | 0/8 | FAIL |
| 8. Legal & Compliance | 0/3 | FAIL |
| 9. Trust Infrastructure | 0/5 | FAIL |
| 10. Launch Day Readiness | 0/5 | FAIL |
| **TOTAL** | **4/51** | **NOT LAUNCH-READY** |

---

## Top 5 Critical Blockers (Fix These First)

1. **Legal** — Privacy Policy and ToS with AI disclaimer must be live before any public users arrive. This is a legal requirement, not a nice-to-have.
2. **Payments** — Stripe in test mode means you cannot take money. Activate live keys and test all flows end-to-end.
3. **Free tier AI cost cap** — Without a request cap on free users, a successful launch could generate a surprise AI bill before you can react.
4. **Auth & error tracking** — If auth or the AI engine breaks on launch day with no Sentry, you'll learn about it from 1-star reviews, not your dashboard.
5. **Pricing page** — You cannot launch a freemium product without a live, scannable pricing page. Users will not upgrade to something they cannot find.

---

## One Bright Spot

Your AI unit economics are genuinely excellent. At $0.20/user/month in AI costs against $9.99 revenue, your margin structure is far healthier than most AI-first consumer apps. The business model works — the product just isn't ready to launch yet.

assertions.text: The social/launch section recommends X, TikTok, Instagram, or YouTube as primary channels for a B2C consumer app — not LinkedIn or GitHub.
assertions.reasoning: The output is truncated mid-Section 7 ('Social Media & La...') so the platform recommendations are never shown, making it impossible to confirm X, TikTok, Instagram, or YouTube were recommended.

assertions.text: The audit uses explicit PASS/FAIL labels for individual items in a structured checklist format.
assertions.reasoning: The audit consistently uses explicit [PASS] and [FAIL] labels for every individual checklist item across all visible sections.

assertions.text: The audit includes a dedicated AI economics section that checks AI costs against the < 30% of revenue threshold.
assertions.reasoning: Section 4 is explicitly titled 'AI Economics' and includes a dedicated check verifying AI costs are 2.0% of premium revenue, well under the stated 30% threshold.

assertions.text: The audit includes a dedicated GEO/AI discovery section separate from general SEO, covering AI platform visibility.
assertions.reasoning: Section 5 is explicitly titled 'GEO & AI Discovery' and covers AI platform visibility across ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Claude separately from general SEO concerns.

assertions.text: The audit includes a trust infrastructure section covering uptime monitoring, error tracking, or security headers — framed as trust-as-a-product.
assertions.reasoning: The output is truncated at Section 7 and no trust infrastructure section covering uptime monitoring, error tracking, or security headers appears in the visible output.
